verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Am I to say plainly what your intention was, my most simple-minded friend?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Do you think that we can believe that you unwittingly gave the name of the martyr to the book of a man who was a heretic, and thus made the ignorant, through their trust in Christ’s witness, become the defenders of Origen?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Considering the erudition for which you are renowned, for which you are praised throughout the West as an illustrious litterateur, so that the men of your party all speak of you as their Coryphæus, I will not suppose that you are ignorant of Eusebius’ Catalogue, which states the fact that the martyr Pamphilus never wrote a single book.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Eusebius himself, the lover and companion of Pamphilus, and the herald of his praises, wrote three books in elegant language containing the life of Pamphilus.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: In these he extols other traits of his character with extraordinary encomiums, and praises to the sky his humility; but on his literary interests he writes as follows in the third book: “What lover of books was there who did not find a friend in Pamphilus?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If he knew of any of them being in want of the necessaries of life, he helped them to the full extent of his power.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He would not only lend them copies of the Holy Scriptures to read, but would give them most readily, and that not only to men, but to women also if he saw that they were given to reading.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He therefore kept a store of manuscripts, so that he might be able to give them to those who wished for them whenever occasion demanded.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He himself however, wrote nothing whatever of his own, except private letters which he sent to his friends, so humble was his estimate of himself.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But the treatises of the old writers he studied with the greatest diligence, and was constantly occupied in meditation upon them.”
